data.title: JPMorgan Chase: The Warning May Be More Important Than The Earnings
data.description:
The last article on JPMorgan Chase (JPM) warned about the economic situation. In the latest quarterly report, it was reiterated that the bank is ready for a wide variety of scenarios. The reason for this is economics does not have a time variable in nearly any research. What would appear to happen is people and the market fill in what they believe will provide certainty by giving uncertainty a deadline. But that often proves to be a very dead end. It is probably a better investment strategy to deal with the uncertainty rather than allow an artificial deadline to pass (and thereby declare the uncertainty ended).

One thing that Jamie Dimon, Chairman & CEO, has stated is that the consumer is unusually resilient in spending. Right now, this is being attributed to the stimulatory effects of the "big, beautiful bill". However, at the same time, there is a warning about the government debt being piled up. He also stated that we likely will not deal with that debt issue until there is a crisis.

This goes back to my idea that Americans love to borrow money. But they hate to pay it back (because of what it takes to pay it back). Therefore, despite the debt pileup, expanding the deficit seems to be a popular political move until the day comes when the debt market is "done". That could potentially turn out to be a big wake-up call for voters. Jamie Dimon stated that the United States was not the only country with big deficits. But this country is the one to be worried about because we all live here.

How we resolve our future challenges and when we choose to resolve them will have a major effect on banking in his eyes. Because of this, the future outlook appears to be far more important than is the case for current earnings.

Earnings

This bank likely outperformed the industry again, and it will likely continue to outperform the industry in the long-term as long as Jamie Dimon is in charge.

There was a mention of increasing depreciation of auto loans that played a factor in some of the consumer (line of business) results. That may turn out to be a proactive move that mitigates a change in the banking environment later.

But overall, the bank has not yet seen an increase in expenses that would indicate some headwinds. As noted, before, there are definitely threats that may present themselves as tough challenges for this industry. But right now, they are not bothering either Mr. Market or really the consumer.

An interesting take from the bank's (management's) viewpoint is that gasoline prices are roughly 3% of the consumer budget (probably before the increase in prices). So far, the consumer appears to be dealing with the price increase by decreasing discretionary spending elsewhere. Loans do not yet appear to be affected.

It was also stressed that the consumer does well when the labor market is in good shape. As far as the bank management was concerned, the labor market was in "good enough" shape that no models were changed to account for more risk.

Private Credit

Jamie Dimon has been asked both in interviews and during the conference call about private credit.

He has not varied on his view of the situation that private credit will suffer much worse than the banking system for some of the reasons stated above (and probably a few more).

Private credit has made the news from time to time, which has brought out this question to the banks. But banks have a lot of regulatory hurdles for any business that they do. Therefore, deteriorating loan values are likely marked to market well ahead of time. Many also have a workout department to deal with the worst-case scenario.

I think that the prevailing view is that the banks have a lot of regulatory safeguards that the private lending industry does not have. That can vary because insurance companies have their own regulations to deal with depending upon the situation (for example).

But some of the recent headlines highlight the fact that banking or lending in any form depends upon the trust of the people that give the institutions money. That trust can change "overnight". That is what makes the Federal Reserve Board (and FDIC) so important. It gives the average person trust whereas a private situation may not have that trust. While banks have ways to potentially avoid a disastrous "run", some of those ways are not available to some private entities.

Matching is a very big deal in banking. Anything that undoes those matching assumptions in a significant way is a threat to the business.

Summary

There have long been warnings about several challenges facing the industry in the future. One of the biggest warnings has got to be that asset values are high (historically speaking). This warning has been out for some time now.

The conference call questions seem to indicate a lot of concerns about the future (and how that affects banking).

But economics does not have a time factor. Therefore, what will unfold in the future is uncertain and depends upon what happens in the meantime. One of the biggest investing mistakes any investor can make is to try to force certainty on an inherently uncertain situation. It is usually related to economics.

JPMorgan Chase remains a strong buy because this bank, more than most, has an excellent position in the market. That position should allow it to continue to outperform many competitors (even other money center banks). The diversification involved in being a money center bank also helps for whatever lies ahead.

The business itself is doing well long after many thought trouble would appear. But those of us who are old enough to remember the 1970s may well remember how complacent everything and everybody got until trouble struck. The economy was a real challenge until the 1980s as a result.

Many of us can tell that pressure is building in the wrong direction at the current time. But most of us also know that usually things can be mitigated or fixed entirely if taken care of before that pressure presents itself as a challenge. The real question is when does it become politically palatable to deal with economic (or any other) situations before there is a crisis.

Risks

Probably the biggest risk would be the loss of services of Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO. He would be a huge challenge to replace were something to happen to him. That may well cause an immediate re-evaluation of the bank's future.

Lately the increasing deficit combined with low-interest rates is classic economics for inflationary pressures to build. Now, if and when that pressure presents itself as the actual challenge of inflation is another matter entirely. It would definitely put some pressure on the bank's matching process.

Consumer spending is seen as remarkably resilient right now. If and when that changes, it may require an outlook change for banking in general.

Similarly, consumer debt seems to be in good shape as well. If that changes, it could affect the economic outlook as well as the banking outlook.

Right now the focus appears to be on private debt. But that may turn out to be "the tip of the iceberg". It could well end up signaling major challenges ahead. Right now, we really do not know.

data.title: AIO: Not Structurally Built To Efficiently Participate In AI Growth
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Virtus Artificial Intelligence & Technology Opp Fd (AIO), I issued a hold rating due to the fund's efficiency but underperformance against peers. However, the fund has released an updated annual report, and market conditions have shifted since my last coverage. Therefore, I wanted to revisit AIO to take a more focused look at its performance, outlook, and overall value proposition. AIO provides direct exposure to some of the highest-quality companies in the world while also implementing a blended strategy with some income-producing securities.

When I previously covered AIO, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 10.67%. Despite the market's rally over the last week, AIO still trades at a similar level with a current discount to NAV of 10.69%. For reference, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.78% over the last five-year period. Therefore, this larger-than-average discount level may be an indication that it is an attractive time to begin accumulating shares. I am optimistic about the outlook of the market, and I think AIO is positioned to capitalize on the expansion of AI.

AIO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.7% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. The latest annual report indicates that the fund is certainly capable of producing earnings that outpace those distributions. However, the focus on income has some tradeoffs that investors must consider. Furthermore, the fund utilizes leverage, and this introduces some sensitivity to interest rates. Ultimately, AIO can be a solid holding for income investors, but the fund's structure means that upside growth is limited, even during market rallies.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, AIO now has total managed assets of $1.04B that are spread across a blended portfolio of assets. For instance, the fund aims to invest at least 80% of its net assets into securities that are focused on artificial intelligence companies and stand to benefit from the expansion of the technology sector. The fund takes a multi-asset approach, which helps it achieve both capital appreciation and income generation through shifting market conditions. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 49.91%
  • Convertible Securities: 32.25%
  • High-Yield Bonds: 15.30%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 2.54%

Convertible securities essentially operate as a bond-like instrument that allows AIO to collect interest income. However, management has the ability to convert these securities to common shares if they believe there is substantial growth potential. Since AIO is actively managed, the fund doesn't try to replicate any specific index. Instead, the holdings are hand-picked by management. Furthermore, it's important to note that AIO is a term fund with an expected termination around October of 2031.

When looking at the portfolio's breakdown by sector, we can see that AIO is most heavily allocated to information technology companies at 45.28% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrials and health care investments, which accounts for 18.16% and 8.03% of assets, respectively. Therefore, the fund isn't technically 100% dedicated to the AI companies in the way you may expect. This added diversity can help provide some better price stability and lower volatility.

As you would expect, AIO also provides exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. For instance, NVIDIA (NVDA) is the largest position at 5.11% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to other notable companies, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Lumentum Holdings (LITE), Broadcom (AVGO), and Welltower (WELL), just to name a few. We can also see that some of these securities are held as convertible securities, so the fund may not be able to entirely participate in their upside growth.

Disappointing NAV Growth & Risks

Since the time of my last coverage, AIO released an updated annual report that provides us with transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.06 per share, while net realized gains were much higher at $4.27 per share. Looking back on prior years, the fund's net investment income has been pretty lackluster, especially for a fund that prioritizes distributions for shareholders. This is one of my biggest issues with AIO because if the portfolio has convertible securities and high-yield bonds, the net investment income should be much higher. The poor performance in this aspect of its earnings can be an indication that the fund's fees are high and chewing into the operating margins.

Furthermore, the fund utilizes leverage as a way to amplify its earnings when possible. However, the use of leverage can also be a contributing factor as to why the net investment income levels are so poor. Since interest rates are high, the spread between AIO's cost of leverage and the yield collected from positions is thin. The fund now has effective leverage totaling 12.44% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $130M. Although a mild amount of leverage, this can still amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated.

The current structure of the fund also reveals that AIO is heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions. While this is okay, it does mean that AIO is vulnerable to market declines. During periods of market downturns, the fund is likely to see net realized losses but still pay out a large distribution. Therefore, AIO can find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earned, which was the case in 2022. These large NAV declines can severely impact the rate of NAV growth. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $29.20 per share
  • 2022: $24.18 per share
  • 2023: $19.92 per share
  • 2024: $20.61 per share
  • 2025: $23.52 per share
  • 2026: $24.86 per share

Since the fund is essentially paying out most of its earnings as a distribution, AIO is forced to sell off the upside of its strongest positions. For investors, this translates to limited capital appreciation potential since AIO's positions aren't capable of growing over time without interruption. For instance, AIO's share price has declined by a little more than 15% since inception, despite the market indices racing to all-time highs over the last few years. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 53.4% since inception. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the distributions being paid.

Due to the fund's flawed strategy and results, I want to emphasize that AIO only makes the most sense for investors that specifically want to focus on income. If you are an investor who prefers to maximize total return potential from the growth of AI, you would be better off with a more traditional ETF like the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ). As we can see below, AIO severely underperforms against AIQ since its inception, despite offering a high dividend yield.

Outlook

Since the fund is focused on companies that can benefit from the growth of AI, AIO is aligned to see some upside potential over the next few years. AI continues to be a large driver of earnings growth for businesses, and I am optimistic about the outlook of the sector. However, AIO is certainly not the most efficient way to maintain exposure to the growth of the sector due to its structural limitations. So despite my optimism on AI, I believe that AIO is likely to continue underperforming over a longer holding period.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can reach a value of ~$3.5T by 2033. This would represent a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% over the next few years. However, AIO has to actively sell off the upside growth of its holdings to fuel distributions, so the fund may not be able to fully capture the growth of the sector. Furthermore, AIO's diversity across other sectors makes the fund a lot less focused on AI growth.

With that being said, AIO isn't the best approach for capitalizing on this growth. Additionally, if we ever face a scenario where the growth of AI slows, the fund is likely to see its NAV continue to erode. If AIO isn't capable of capturing a high level of net realized gains, the fund may find itself in a scenario where it is frequently paying out more than it actually earns.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.15 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.7%. Assuming the fund keeps distributions at the same level over the next twelve months, we can estimate that AIO will issue an annual payout total of $1.80 per share. Referring to the same annual report, AIO has demonstrated that it is capable of generating earnings that outpace the distributions being paid. For instance, the fund reported total earnings of $4.33 per share for its latest period, which was strong enough to support several years worth of distributions.

2026's earnings of $4.33 per share / $1.80 annual payout = ~2.4 years.

Looking back over the dividend history, the fund has managed to keep payouts within a consistent range. Given its ability to produce excess income, this comes as no surprise. You will also notice that the fund is capable of issuing large supplemental payouts when earnings are strong. For instance, AIO issued a year-end supplemental distribution of $1.191 per share because of the large net realized gains that were captured from the market's rally. If the AI market continues to expand, it is certainly possible for AIO to reward investors with another supplemental at the end of this year.

Since the starting yield is already so high and the fund can provide supplementals, investors should not anticipate any sort of traditional dividend growth over a longer holding period. Despite this, the consistent payout range should still be commended because it allows investors to create their own dividend growth over time. This can be achieved by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital over time. When you also consider that coverage is strong, this makes AIO a great option for investors seeking income reliability.

To demonstrate the strength of AIO as an income tool, I ran a backtest of an initial $100,000 investment at inception. This backtest assumes that no additional capital was ever added after the initial $100k. However, the back test does assume that all distributions received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. By simply remaining invested, you would have seen a lumpy but large rise in annual dividend income:

2020 Dividend Income: $12,783

2025 Dividend Income: $27,939

Lastly, AIO does offer investors some slight tax efficiency. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 63.3% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as long-term capital gains, which are qualified dividends and have favorable tax treatment. However, tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily mean tax-free. There is still a portion of distributions paid YTD that were supported by net investment income and short-term capital gains, which are taxed at the highest rates. So while tax consequences may be minimal, some investors may still want to utilize AIO within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I still think that AIO is a hold at this time. Despite the attractive discount to NAV valuation, the fund isn't structurally capable of fully participating in the upside growth of the AI sector. The fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions, which means that AIO's holdings aren't capable of growing over time without interruption. Furthermore, the fund's net investment income levels are poor, especially for a fund that utilizes income-producing securities. While investors can collect a high rate of distributions, AIO is still likely to underperform in the future.

data.title: A First Look At Lyell Immunopharma
data.description:
Shares of cell therapy concern Lyell Immunopharma, Inc. (LYEL) have retreated by around half since spiking to $45 in December 2025 after being upgraded by HC Wainwright and Seeking Alpha’s PQP. Those moves were somewhat triggered by a company presentation of updated positive data on its lead candidate, ronde-cel, treating relapsed-refractory large B-cell lymphoma (R/R LBCL) at ASH. With a second promising candidate (LYL273) against third-line or later metastatic colorectal cancer in-licensed from Chinese biotech ICT in Nov 2025, the name merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Lyell Immunopharma, Inc., is a South San Francisco, California-based clinical-stage biotechnology concern focused on the development of autologous (from the patient) chimeric antigen receptor [CAR] T cell therapies for the treatment of cancer. The company is currently advancing two programs against three indications, including lead candidate rondecabtagene autoleucel (ronde-cel) against R/R LBCL. Lyell was founded in 2018 and went public in 2021, raising net proceeds of $391.8 million at $340 per share, after giving effect to a reverse 1-for-20 stock split in June 2025. Its stock trades around $23.50 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of nearly $550 million.

Pipeline

The company removes the patient’s own blood, subjects it to apheresis (centrifuge separation) to isolate its components, including T cells, which are then armed with enhancements to render them more potent cancer-cell eradicators. These CAR T cells are then reintroduced into the patient’s body to destroy tumors. From this approach, Lyell has developed many programs, with two currently advancing through the clinic, one of which was recently in-licensed.

Ronde-cel. The company’s most advanced asset is ronde-cel, an anti-CD19/CD20 CAR T cell therapy designed with an ‘OR’ logic gate to target B cells that express either CD19 or CD20, further enhanced with CD62L-positive cells to promote production of more naïve and central memory CAR T cells, which engender greater persistence and reduced exhaustion for improved antitumor activity, as well as lower adverse cytokine production. It is currently undergoing investigation in the treatment of R/R LBCL.

More specifically, Lyell is conducting a registrational single-arm study (PiNACLE) evaluating ronde-cel in the treatment of third-line or later (3L+) R/R LBCL, as well as a head-to-head Phase 3 trial (PiNACLE-H2H) treating 2L LBCL. The malady afflicts ~30,000 Americans annually, with ~12,000 progressing after 1L treatment—typically R-CHOP, which consists of anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab, chemotherapy, antibiotic doxorubicin, alkaloid vincristine, and corticosteroid prednisone. Approximately 6,500 progress after 2L treatment, which is typically CD19-targeting therapies, including Gilead Sciences’ (GILD) Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel) or Bristol-Myers Squibb’s (BMY) Breyanzi (lisocabtagene maraleucel). Third-line strategies are more anti-CD19s, with Novartis’ (NVS) Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel) added to the mix. Second-line treatments have a median progression-free survival (mPFS) expectation of 9-15 months, while 3L therapies have mPFS of three to seven months. CAR T cell products treating LBCL are expected to generate global sales of $3.9 billion in FY26.

Lyell is confident of ronde-cel’s chances for approval in the 3L+ setting after the therapy demonstrated a 93% overall response rate (ORR), a 76% complete response [CR] rate, and mPFS of 18 months across 29 patients in that cohort in a Phase 1/2 study. The pivotal single-arm trial, which is an extension of the Phase 1/2 study, is enrolling ~120 patients, with a clinical update anticipated in 2H26. Potentially registrational data, including the primary endpoint of best overall response rate, are expected in mid-2027, with a BLA submission to follow in 2H27.

As for PiNACLE-H2H, Lyell is enrolling up to 400 2L LBCL patients, who will be randomized 1:1 to receive either ronde-cel or the investigator’s choice of Yescarta or Breyanzi. No timeline for data has been forwarded for this pivotal data. To date, in addition to superior efficacy in 3L+, ronde-cel has provided a better safety profile than the other two approved therapies, with no Grade 3 or greater cytokine release syndrome (versus 3% for Breyanzi and 9% for Yescarta) and 4% (1/25) Grade 3 or greater neurotoxicity (as compared to 10% for Breyanzi and 31% for Yescarta).

Ronde-cel has received Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy and Fast Track designations from the FDA.

LYL273. The company’s other program is LYL273, a guanylyl cyclase C (GCC)-targeting CAR T-cell candidate enhanced with a CD19 CAR T with controlled cytokine release that is undergoing evaluation for the treatment of GCC-expressing cancers. LYL273 is essentially three CAR T cells (GCC, CD19, and GCC/CD19 doublet) wrapped into one, with its first clinical target metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC), the second-most deadly cancer in the U.S. with 50,000 deaths, and 150,000 new diagnoses per annum, and GCC expression in 95% of patients. Drugs treating this disease generated global sales north of $7 billion in 2025.

If surgery is not an option, mCRC is generally treated with some form of doublet [CAPOX] or triplet (Folfox) chemotherapy with or without immunotherapy such as Merck’s (MRK) anti-PD-L1 mega-blockbuster Keytruda (pembrolizumab) or other targeted therapies such as anti-vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR), anti-epidermal growth factor receptor, or other 5-fluorouracil-based regimens. The 3L+ setting of this cancer is an area of high unmet need, currently treated by a combination of Taiho Oncology’s chemotherapy double-agent Lonsurf plus Roche’s (RHHBY) monoclonal IgG1 antibody Avastin (bevacizumab); Takeda’s (TAK) inhibitor of VEGFR-1, -2, and -3 Fruzaqla (fruquintinib); Lonsurf monotherapy; or Bayer’s (BAYZF) multi-kinase inhibitor Stivarga (regorafenib). They have very poor ORRs, ranging from 1% to 6%, and median overall survival [MOS] of only six to eleven months. That said, Exelixis (EXEL) has submitted an NDA for its multi-kinase candidate zanzalintinib in combination with Roche’s PD-L1 antibody Tecentriq (atezolizumab) for treating microsatellite stable mCRC (95% of the indication) with a PDUFA date of December 3, 2026. That said, the combo only produced mPFS of 3.7 months in its pivotal trial (STELLAR-303).

With that as the backdrop, Lyell is conducting a Phase 1 trial assessing LYL273 in the treatment of mCRC, which has shown promise, demonstrating a 67% (4/6) ORR and mPFS of 8 months at dose level 2. A third dose level is enrolling with no dose-limiting toxicities observed to date and more data anticipated in 1H26 and 2H26.

The candidate was onboarded from Chinese concern Innovative Cellular Therapeutics (ICT) in November 2025 after dose level 2 delivered an ORR of 57% (4/7), mOS of 25 months, and mPFS of 6.0 months in a proof-of-concept trial out of China that was published in JAMA Oncology during 2024. In return for the global rights to LYL273 outside of China, Hong Kong, Macau, and Taiwan, Lyell paid ICT $40 million upfront, issued 1.9 million shares of LYEL (currently 8% of the shares outstanding), and is potentially obligated for clinical, regulatory, and commercial milestones totaling $820 million plus 1.85 million additional shares, as well as mid-single-digit to 10% royalties. Lyell anticipates initiating a pivotal trial for LYL273 treating mCRC in 1H27 with an eye on pursuing pancreatic cancer, which expresses GCC in 60% of patients.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To advance these two programs, the company held cash and securities of $247.2 million at YE25, providing it an operating runway into 2Q27. On March 6, 2026, Lyell extended that runway by exercising a put provision in a July 2025 private placement, providing it with an additional $50 million at $25.61 per share. This transaction raised beneficial owner Arch Venture Fund’s ownership interest to ~28%.

The company’s small Street following leans positive, as can be seen above. On average, sell-side analysts anticipate ronde-cel to hit the market in 2028 and generate sales of $190 million in 2029.

Verdict:

When Lyell provided the ronde-cel update at the 67th American Society of Hematology Annual Meeting on December 7, 2025, it motivated HC Wainwright to upgrade shares of LYEL from hold to buy on December 9th. Also, an 8-K dropped on December 19th that provided additional detail on recently in-licensed LYL273. The continuing price momentum triggered Lyell’s stock’s addition to the Seeking Alpha PRO Quant Portfolio on December 22nd, the day its thinly traded shares peaked at an 18-month high of $45. They have since slumped over 45%.

Reverse stock splits are not due to past successes, and Lyell has plenty of clinical cell therapy failures in its wake, including GSK (GSK)-partnered programs LYL132 and LYL331, which were terminated in 2022; ROR1-targeting LYL797; tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte candidate LYL845; and preclinical program LYL119, all of which were terminated in 2024. However, after a long series of misses, Lyell appears to have an approval asset with ronde-cel treating 3L+ R/R LBCL and significant upside optionality with LYL797. Trading at only a ~$300 million premium to cash due to the recent sharp pullback provides a decent entry point into Lyell.

However, LYEL only merits a small position for aggressive, risk-tolerant investors, given the company is not projected to be profitable until early next decade. Given my cautious view on the overall market, I am not putting LYEL into my personal portfolio right now.

data.title: Ophir Asset Management March 2026 Letter To Investors
data.description:
The 5 Most Important Charts Defining Markets Today

I must say, it’s been tough choosing when to sit down and write this Letter. With a new rolling deadline for the Iran war always “just hours away”, I kept putting it off.

A thought invariably rolled through my mind: “Before putting pen to paper, let’s wait for the arrival of the next big market-moving news.”

The reality, though, is in a one-variable market – with daily market moves almost solely dictated by war headlines – it’s best just to get on with it.

Why? Because the end of the war could be this week… or it could still be months away.

(HINT: As we’ll elaborate later, it’s more likely weeks than months. Polymarket has a 78% chance that Trump will announce the end of military operations against Iran by 30 June.)

But our edge is not trying to ‘outguess’ the millions of other investors about Trump’s next tweet; our edge is analysing small-cap companies, where few people are looking, through intensive research and travel.

So, while our Ophir Funds were down fairly in line with the market/their benchmarks in March, that’s ok with us.

Every one of the circa 80 global and regional share market indices we track were down in March due to the war, so it was hard to avoid!

(For us to have materially outperformed last month, we would have had to change our style from Growth to Value, and buy energy, financials, telcos and utilities! That’s not quite our core style.)

In this month’s Letter, we share five charts that have really caught our attention over the past month and helped shape our thinking about markets.

But first, thank the bond market for a happy TACO-versary

Just over a year ago, on his so-called ‘Liberation Day,’ April 2, 2025, Trump tabled a broad package of reciprocal tariffs. Share markets plummeted. A few days later, Trump retreated and announced a 90-day grace period on tariffs.

What caused the about-face?

It’s widely speculated that he got a tap on the shoulder from his Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, who saw the 10-year bond yield shoot up to 4.5% and warned it would worsen the US’s already ballooning debt costs and fiscal deficit.

When you have US$39 trillion in government debt and are adding a US$2 trillion fiscal deficit to it every year, higher interest rates are like pouring kerosene on an already raging inferno.

Fast forward almost exactly a year, and on April 7, Trump warned he would obliterate Iran and a “whole civilisation will die” if Iran didn’t open the Strait of Hormuz. Then, a day later, he agreed to a last-minute two-week ceasefire.

As higher oil prices stoked inflation and rate rise worries, the 10-year bond yield rose from around 3.9% at the start of the war in late February to over 4.4% in late March.

Chart 1. TACOnomics

When the 10-year (or Bessent) speaks, Trump listens.

Again, it was the bond market and higher yields that caused Trump to TACO and not let the war go on too long.

It reminds us again of that famous quote by Bill Clinton’s political advisor, James Carville:

“I used to think that if there was reincarnation, I wanted to come back as the President or the Pope or as a .400 baseball hitter. But now I would like to come back as the bond market. You can intimidate everybody.”

Everybody, including Donald Trump.

We think this is more likely to mean, like most geopolitical events, that the negative share market impact is relatively short lived before the recovery takes hold.

Charts 2 & 3. Margin of Safety

For small caps, this will not be a repeat of the 2022 oil spike.

While the war may not drag on too long, small-cap investors could be forgiven for having PTSD. The current oil price spike, after all, might remind them of 2022.

Almost exactly four years before the US and Israel launched coordinated military operations on Iran, Russia (the world’s third-largest oil producer) invaded Ukraine.

This led to a sequence of events you can see in the chart below. Oil (brown line) spiked and fuelled already-rising post-COVID inflation.

Both short-term interest rates (controlled by central banks) and long-term interest rates (controlled by the market) started rocketing higher. The gold line shows the US 10-year bond yield (the same one TACO’ing Trump in the first chart in this Letter).

Already sensing that rates would rise during the COVID recovery, equity markets had begun selling off in 2021, but the falls continued in 2022 on the back of the oil spike. You can see this for both US large caps (green line) and small caps (orange line) in the chart below, where we highlight the fall in their P/E ratios.

You can also clearly see that small-cap valuations fell much more – as we were painfully aware here at Ophir!

(All the while, the recession probability was rising in the US (inverted blue line) and peaked at over 60% in late 2022/2023.)

Which brings us to THE question.

Should we be afraid that today’s high oil prices are going to cause a replay sell-off of small caps?

If oil prices stay high enough for long enough, it’s certainly a possibility that central banks may have to raise interest rates to combat higher inflation, increasing the risk of recession and share market falls.

Markets today, however, are not really expecting that scenario for a couple of key reasons:

  1. 2022 was an oil price spike on top of already high and rising inflation from COVID. By contrast, this time, prior to the Iran war, US inflation had been falling, and there were expectations of Fed rate cuts, not hikes.
  2. Essentially, this current spike in oil is self-imposed by the US. It can wind down military operations and relieve pressure on the oil price.

In many ways, Trump will have to weigh a long, drawn-out war (if needed to reach objectives in Iran) against the economic cost to the US (higher inflation and interest rates and potential recession).

Most suspect that, because it will likely cost him dearly at the mid-term elections in November, Trump won’t think the cost of a long war is worth bearing.

That is likely why recession probabilities haven’t spiked and why the market is not yet pricing in rate hikes by the Federal Reserve.

Moreover, with a P/E of around 15x, US small-cap valuations are relatively cheap and well below the more expensive 22-23x before the sell-off in 2021.

Given that the lowest small caps have reached in the last three bear markets are around 11-12x, small caps today have a lot more downside protection and margin of safety.

Chart 4. Nvidia’s Crude Awakening

This next chart shows one of the craziest stats we came across in the last month.

Nvidia’s one-year forward PE: 18.7x.

ExxonMobil: 20.2x.

That’s right, Nvidia stock is now cheaper than ExxonMobil.

Let that sink in.

The company supplying the picks and shovels for the AI gold rush – the most important technology buildout of our lifetime – trades at a lower one-year forward P/E (price-to-earnings ratio) than an oil major that has roots back to the 1870s!

At Ophir, we watched Nvidia’s meteoric rise and kept asking ourselves: when does the valuation gravity eventually kick in?

Well, here we are.

Three years ago, the market was willing to pay almost anything for AI, and at a P/E of 60x had priced Nvidia to perfection and then some.

Today, despite expecting Nvidia’s earnings to almost double this financial year from US$113 billion to US$203 billion, the market is now pricing Nvidia more like a utility.

It’s not that Nvidia’s share price is down a lot that’s causing its valuation to fall – it’s only about 12% off its all-time highs. The market is just having a hard time maintaining Nvidia’s P/E. If the market did maintain the P/E, Nvidia’s market cap would double to over USD$8 trillion!

Investors are clearly questioning the durability of the AI capex cycle and whether it has gotten ahead of itself.

On the flip side, ExxonMobil’s P/E averaged 11x over the last 5 years. Then oil shot up on the back of the Iran war, and Exxon is now trading at almost twice that P/E.

We’re not saying Exxon is overvalued. Oil is a critical business and Exxon is no doubt exceptionally well run. But the optics of this comparison say something important about how dramatically sentiment can swing.

Have these two companies’ fortunes really changed that much over the last six months – when Nvidia’s P/E was twice that of Exxon – or is this one of the great mispricings of the decade?

Chart 5. Aussie Small Caps Marked Down

For over a year now, we have been beating the drum about how cheap US and global small caps have been versus large caps.

But one of the great untold stories of the last couple of months is just how cheap Aussie small caps have become.

At a 13.9x price-to-earnings ratio (one-year forward earnings), the only two other times they have been this cheap in the last decade are:

  1. March 2020, when the COVID sell-off hit.
  2. Very briefly in June 2022, during the fastest global rate-hiking cycle in 40 years, when the consensus call from economists was recession.

Perhaps even more stark is Australian small caps’ valuation relative to Australian large caps.

Over the last 20 years, Aussie small caps have traded at an average P/E premium to large caps of +0.7x.

Today they are trading almost 4 P/E points lower – the LOWEST in the 20 years of data available.

A big cause has been the RBA rate-hike cycle. It started in early February after a sticky inflation reading for the December 2025 quarter (released 28th January 2026).

Since that late-January CPI report, the ASX Small Ordinaries index fell -16.6% to the end of March, while the ASX 50 index has fallen just -2.4%.

With Aussie small-cap valuations at 13.9x, they are now only marginally above the 13x they bottomed at after the 2022 sell-off, and the 12x they bottomed at in COVID.

So it appears much of the RBA hiking cycle and Iran war fears are priced in.

Are they at their lows?

Who knows.

But, absent any unforeseen events, they historically haven’t been much lower.

And as the research tells us: the single best predictor of an asset class’s return over the long term is its starting valuation.

And on that score, Aussie small caps have now started to look cheap, especially compared to Aussie large caps.

As Buffett said: “Whether we’re talking about socks or stocks, I like buying quality merchandise when it is marked down”.

We think that applies well to Aussie small caps today. It’s our job here at Ophir to find the quality merchandise within small caps.

Thank you for entrusting your capital with us.

Kindest regards,

Andrew Mitchell & Steven Ng

Co-Founders & Senior Portfolio Managers

Ophir Asset Management
